figures.caption: Fig. 5: Animation results of UNICA demonstrating avatar response to key presses. For visualization clarity, we sample one frame every three frames along the trajectory.

Fig. 6: FVD scores over a 2000-frame autoregressively generated sequence, computed using a sliding window of 200 frames with a stride of 10. The red dashed line indicates the linear trend, and the gray shaded region denotes ±1 standard deviation. The nearflat trend demonstrates stable generation quality over extended rollouts.

Fig. 3: The position map rendering process and visualization of a four-frame group. (a) We use an A-Pose mesh of the avatar as geometry and the vertex coordinates of the posed avatar as vertex colors to render position maps. The position maps are rendered from six orthogonal views. (b) We partition the motion sequence into groups of four frames and normalize each group.

Fig. 4: Demonstration of progressive 4D inference. During autoregressive inference of UNICA, each round generates a relative movement that is accumulated in 3D space for the actual movement of the 3D avatar. The output frame of round n will be renormalized before it is used as input for round n+ 1.

Fig. 1. To the best of our knowledge, this work is the first to explore end-to-end action-driven 3D neural avatars. Tab. 1 provides a comparative analysis between UNICA and existing approaches addressing related sub-tasks. Leveraging these capabilities, UNICA facilitates the automatic creation of controllable 3D avatars from light-stage captures.
Table 1: Comparison between UNICA and prior approaches.
Fig. 7: Qualitative comparison between UNICA and baseline methods across different frames and viewpoints.

Fig. I: Paired key-rendering results of avatar sprint start. Starting from a standing pose, the avatar starts to run forward under the key [W]. The sequence is arranged from left to right, top to bottom.

Fig. 2: The pipeline of UNICA. UNICA consists of an action-conditioned multi-frame diffusion model for avatar geometry and a point transformer for point-to-3DGS appearance mapping. The diffusion model takes latents of three position maps as context and generates one subsequent position map conditioned on a chosen action embedding. The generated position map is upscaled using bilinear interpolation and combined with other base 3DGS attributes to form a coarse 3DGS. The point transformer then refines the coarse 3DGS into one with avatar-specific appearance details.
